nativeRange.habitat: Rivers and streams with slow-moving water, often found among aquatic vegetation and woody debris. Native to the middle Congo River basin, including Pool Malebo and the Kasai and Ubangi drainages, as well as the Kouilou-Niari basin.

diet.notes: Readily accepts sinking catfish pellets, flake food, and frozen or live foods such as bloodworm and brine shrimp. Has been documented grazing on algae and biofilm from the undersides of leaves and surfaces, feeding in its characteristic inverted posture. Vegetable supplements like peas, cucumber, and zucchini are accepted.

goodWith: other peaceful community fish of similar size

imageCandidates.notes: John P. Friel Ph.D.; 1280x960. Crisp full lateral view of a live individual showing the marbled/mottled brown blotching, countershaded body and long maxillary barbels — best clean ID shot; minor floating specks in tank water but fish is sharp.

Uploader-credited PD aquarium photo; 1093x2539. Live fish against glass with bold, vibrant dark rosette blotches on a tan body clearly showing diagnostic markings and barbels; sharp, though tail is cropped so kept as a supporting shot.
Neale Monks; 799x574. Whole live fish resting inverted under driftwood in a cave — good behavioral/husbandry context showing mottled pattern; somewhat dark and lower resolution, so non-recommended.

summary: Unique freshwater catfish from the Congo basin that typically swims and feeds inverted — its dark belly and pale back provide countershading camouflage in this unusual orientation. Generally peaceful and best kept in groups of 4 or more.
careNotes: The defining trait of this species is its habitual upside-down swimming posture, which it maintains for up to 90% of the time. This inversion is reflected in its pigmentation: the belly is darker than the back, a reversal of the countershading pattern common in most fish — providing camouflage from below when feeding at the water surface or on the undersides of leaves. The lateral line is oriented to function effectively in the inverted position. In the aquarium, it has been observed feeding at the surface upside-down, scraping algae and biofilm from the undersides of broad leaves, and accepting sinking foods in its inverted posture. Provide caves, PVC tubes, and overhanging decor where the fish can rest inverted — this is normal resting behavior, not illness. Keep in groups of at least 4–6 for confident, visible behavior; lone individuals tend to hide. Sister species such as S. multipunctatus, S. petricola, and S. eupterus typically swim upright and should not be confused with this species. Crepuscular by nature (most active at dawn and dusk), though activity tends to increase with group size and when the fish are well-settled. Copper-based medications should be avoided as with most scaleless catfish.
breedingNotes: Rarely achieved in the aquarium. Cold water changes of around 5°C have been reported to trigger spawning. Females can produce up to around 450 eggs per spawn. Fry are reported to begin adopting the characteristic inverted swimming posture at approximately 7–8 weeks of age. Detailed captive breeding reports are scarce.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary source after Aquarium Co-Op (slice default) returned 404 for this species. SeriouslyFish provides the most complete husbandry profile for this species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (9.6 cm TL), temperature range (22–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), native range (Congo basin including Pool Malebo, Kasai, Ubangi drainages, and Kouilou-Niari basin), and taxonomy (family Mochokidae, order Siluriformes). Temperature averaged with SeriouslyFish (24–28°C) to give 23–27°C. pH upper bound conservatively taken from SeriouslyFish (7.5) per user brief rather than FishBase's 8.0.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Confirmed lifespan range (5–10 years), peaceful temperament, social grouping requirement, and inverted swimming behavior. No specific parameter values provided by this source.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Cold water changes may trigger spawning, with females capable of producing up to around 450 eggs per spawn. Fry begin adopting the characteristic inverted swimming posture at approximately 7–8 weeks of age.
